response: {"image_description":"A square if you cut out a bunch of shapes from it. From the bottom left corner, cut off a small 30-60-90 triangle, with the long side corresponding to the right side. Next to that cut out, cut out a trapezoid, where the bottom left of the trapezoid is right next to the bottom right of the previously cut out triangle. The trapezoid will be tall, with the parallel sides being vertical, with a slight right lean.  The long side (base) of the trapezoid extends from next to the first cut triangle up to the halfway point of the square. It should end to the left of the absolute middle of the square. The slanted side of the trapezoid extends back down south east, ending at the same height as the first cut triangle. Extend the next line parallel to the first side of the trapezoid all the way to the bottom. Then cut out a short, obtuse triangle directly to the right of the trapezoid, with the long side corresponding to the bottom edge of the square. This triangle should be the remaining bottom length of the square, and should come up below the top point of the first triangle. From the right side of the square, cut out two small rectangles at around the midline. From the top, split the cut outs to the left and right sides. From the left side, the top corner is cut off. From the right side, a large right triangle is cut out, another 30-60-90, with the hypotenuse being the top side, and the long side extending from the middle of the top side, south east, to meet the short side that starts at the right top corner of the square. From the middle of the long, adjacent side of that 30-60-90 triangle, cut a small obtuse triangle out of the center of the square, where one of the corners overlaps with the cutout of the 30-60-90 triangle. Overall, the shape is vaguely birdlike. "}
text_response: A square if you cut out a bunch of shapes from it. From the bottom left corner, cut off a small 30-60-90 triangle, with the long side corresponding to the right side. Next to that cut out, cut out a trapezoid, where the bottom left of the trapezoid is right next to the bottom right of the previously cut out triangle. The trapezoid will be tall, with the parallel sides being vertical, with a slight right lean.  The long side (base) of the trapezoid extends from next to the first cut triangle up to the halfway point of the square. It should end to the left of the absolute middle of the square. The slanted side of the trapezoid extends back down south east, ending at the same height as the first cut triangle. Extend the next line parallel to the first side of the trapezoid all the way to the bottom. Then cut out a short, obtuse triangle directly to the right of the trapezoid, with the long side corresponding to the bottom edge of the square. This triangle should be the remaining bottom length of the square, and should come up below the top point of the first triangle. From the right side of the square, cut out two small rectangles at around the midline. From the top, split the cut outs to the left and right sides. From the left side, the top corner is cut off. From the right side, a large right triangle is cut out, another 30-60-90, with the hypotenuse being the top side, and the long side extending from the middle of the top side, south east, to meet the short side that starts at the right top corner of the square. From the middle of the long, adjacent side of that 30-60-90 triangle, cut a small obtuse triangle out of the center of the square, where one of the corners overlaps with the cutout of the 30-60-90 triangle. Overall, the shape is vaguely birdlike.

response: {"image_description":"Looks like a crude, poly outline of Bluey from that one TV show with angel wings. If  Bluey's bottom half/legs were just one rectangle. Bluey is facing to the left, so the corresponding rectangle is on the left side. The wings extend from halfway down the right side of the body, and is made of two shapes, an equilateral triangle and a diamond. One point of the equilateral triangle is intersecting with Bluey's rectangular body. The triangle is also slightly rotated to the right, so the next point on the triangle would point to the top right. This triangle point is cut off, like the top of the pyramid on the one-dollar bill. The last point of the triangle (the bottom-most one) is where the skinny diamond intersects, such that it's almost vertical, with a skinny end pointing towards the ground."}
